iucnNote: Heavily targeted across the Indo-Pacific; regional stocks assessed as Endangered. Choose farmed or smaller portions where possible.
sizeWeight: 300 g – 4 kg (sold whole or in steaks)
habitat: Shallow coastal and estuarine waters with muddy bottoms
flavour: Sweet, clean and delicate with almost no fishiness
texture: Soft, fine flakes that stay moist; very tender
preparation: Best steamed whole or as a thick cutlet. Pat dry, score the thickest part, and steam just until the flesh turns opaque. Excellent for babies and the elderly because of the soft flesh.
description: Silvery elongated body with a slightly pointed snout and several thread-like filaments trailing from the lower part of the pectoral fin — the namesake 'threads'. Often sold as thick boneless steaks.

iucnNote: Listed as Vulnerable due to intense fishing pressure across its range. A festive favourite, so demand is high — buy sparingly.
habitat: Coastal waters over muddy substrates, often in schools
flavour: Mild, sweet and buttery
texture: Fine, soft and almost boneless flesh
preparation: The classic Teochew treatment: steam whole with salted vegetables, tomato and sour plum. Score both sides lightly so the heat penetrates evenly. Avoid overcooking — the flesh dries out quickly.
description: Distinctly flat, diamond-shaped silvery body with no pelvic fins and a deeply forked tail. The whole fish looks almost circular when viewed side-on.

iucnNote: Near Threatened — a long-lived predator vulnerable to overfishing. Usually sold pre-cut into steaks.
habitat: Open coastal waters, a fast-swimming pelagic predator
flavour: Rich, savoury and slightly oily
texture: Firm, meaty and dense — holds its shape well
preparation: The default fish for sliced fish soup and fish curry. Cut across the grain into thick steaks. Sear or pan-fry for soup, or simmer gently in curry. Its firmness makes it forgiving for beginners.
description: Long torpedo-shaped silvery body with faint wavy vertical bars and small finlets running toward the tail. Almost always displayed as cross-section steaks with a central bone.

iucnNote: Least Concern and widely farmed in Singapore and the region — one of the more sustainable everyday choices.
habitat: Estuaries, mangroves and coastal farms (catadromous)
flavour: Mild and slightly sweet
preparation: Versatile: steam whole with soy and ginger, or fillet for pan-frying. Keep the skin on for crisping. A reliable all-rounder that forgives slight overcooking.
description: Streamlined silvery-grey body with a distinctly concave forehead, large mouth and forked tail. Often sold live in tanks or whole on ice.

iucnNote: Least Concern, though prized and pricey. Increasingly farmed.
habitat: Coral reefs, rocky areas and coastal mangroves
flavour: Sweet and refined, considered a premium steaming fish
preparation: A premium steaming fish — Cantonese style with light soy, ginger and scallion lets its sweetness shine. Larger fish can be cut into thick steaks for curry.
description: Coppery-gold to bronze body, sometimes with a dark spot on each side near the lateral line, with a typical snapper profile: sloping head and a single continuous dorsal fin.

iucnNote: Least Concern and commonly farmed in cages around the region.
habitat: Mangroves and estuaries when young, reefs as adults
flavour: Clean, mildly sweet
preparation: The everyday 'red fish' for assam (tamarind) curry and fish head curry. Robust enough to simmer without falling apart. Also good steamed or fried.
description: Reddish to coppery body, often darker on the back, with a classic snapper shape. The go-to inexpensive red fish in wet markets, frequently sold as a head and collar for curry.

iucnNote: Near Threatened in the wild; much of the market supply is now farmed. Prefer farmed grouper.
habitat: Reefs, rocky areas and estuaries
flavour: Sweet and delicate with a hint of richness
texture: Firm, gelatinous and moist — prized for its silky skin
preparation: A banquet steaming fish. Steam whole with superior soy, ginger and spring onion. The skin and head are gelatinous and treasured. Larger ones make a luxurious fish head curry.
description: Stout, robust body in olive-brown to grey, covered in small orange or brown spots, with a large mouth and rounded tail. Often sold live in tanks.

iucnNote: Least Concern — an abundant, fast-breeding schooling fish and a sustainable budget pick.
habitat: Coastal waters in large schools
flavour: Savoury and slightly oily, full-flavoured
texture: Soft, fine flesh with small bones
preparation: Almost always deep-fried whole until crisp — bones and all — and eaten with sambal or chilli. Marinate with turmeric and salt first. A nasi lemak staple.
description: Small slender silvery fish with a single bright yellow stripe running from the eye to the tail. Sold in heaps by the kilo.

iucnNote: Data Deficient — not formally assessed, but a fast-growing, abundant schooling fish generally regarded as a sound budget choice.
habitat: Coastal waters in dense schools
flavour: Rich, oily and full-flavoured (high in omega-3)
preparation: Grill or fry whole, or simmer in assam pedas (sour-spicy tamarind gravy). Score the sides and rub with turmeric and salt before cooking to tame the oiliness.
description: Compact silvery-blue mackerel with a rounded body, large eyes and a faint golden band along the flank. Smaller and deeper-bodied than the batang.

iucnNote: Least Concern and almost entirely farmed — an affordable, low-impact freshwater fish.
habitat: Freshwater ponds and farms
flavour: Very mild, slightly sweet; can be earthy if pond-raised
preparation: Best deep-fried whole and finished with sweet-sour sauce, or steamed with plenty of ginger and garlic to lift any muddiness. Score deeply so seasoning penetrates.
description: Deep-bodied freshwater fish, grey to reddish (red tilapia is common), with a long spiny dorsal fin and vertical barring. Usually sold live.

iucnNote: Least Concern and extensively farmed across Southeast Asia.
habitat: Coastal and brackish farm ponds
flavour: Mild and slightly sweet, milky as the name suggests
texture: Soft and tender but notoriously bony
preparation: Famous for its many fine bones. Deep-fry until crisp so smaller bones soften, or buy deboned belly. Popular fried and served with sambal, or in soups.
description: Streamlined silvery fish with a large forked tail, small toothless mouth and big eyes covered by a fatty eyelid. Looks sleek and bright silver.

iucnNote: Many wild stingray and ray species are Vulnerable or Endangered due to slow breeding and heavy fishing. Eat occasionally and check the species.
habitat: Sandy and muddy seabeds in coastal waters
flavour: Mild and slightly sweet, soaks up marinades beautifully
texture: Soft, stringy flesh that separates into fibres along the cartilage
preparation: The hawker classic: slather a wing with sambal, wrap in banana leaf and grill (BBQ sambal stingray). The cartilage stays soft and the flesh peels off in strands.
description: Sold as flat, triangular 'wings' of pinkish flesh with visible cartilage ridges fanning out like a hand. The whole ray is a flat diamond shape with a long thin tail.

iucnNote: Least Concern; the most widely farmed prawn in the region. Farmed sourcing varies in sustainability.
habitat: Coastal waters and brackish aquaculture ponds
flavour: Sweet and rich with a deep prawn aroma
preparation: The premium all-purpose prawn. Leave the shell on for grilling, cereal prawns or salted-egg prawns — the shell carries flavour. Devein along the back. Cook just until they curl into a C and turn coral.
description: Large prawn with bold dark grey-and-cream banding across the shell (the 'tiger' stripes), turning bright orange-red when cooked. Long antennae and a prominent rostrum.

iucnNote: Not formally evaluated by the IUCN. A major wild and farmed food prawn across Southeast Asia.
habitat: Estuaries and coastal waters with muddy bottoms
flavour: Clean and sweet, slightly milder than tiger prawns
preparation: The everyday cooking prawn for noodles, sambal and stir-fries. Wonderful in white pepper or cereal prawns. Keep heads on for stock — they hold lots of flavour.
description: Pale yellow-to-translucent prawn with faintly banana-coloured legs and antennae, and a smooth unbanded shell. Slimmer than the tiger prawn.

iucnNote: Least Concern; widely caught and farmed. The big crab behind chilli and black pepper crab.
habitat: Mangroves, estuaries and mudflats
flavour: Sweet, rich meat with a luxurious roe in females
preparation: The icon of Singapore seafood. Buy live and lively (tie the claws). Chop into sections and crack the claws before stir-frying in chilli or black pepper sauce. Females (rounder apron) carry prized orange roe.
description: Large heavy crab with a smooth olive-green to brown shell, enormous powerful claws and the last pair of legs flattened into paddles. Usually sold live with claws bound.

iucnNote: Data Deficient — heavily fished but not formally assessed. A popular, lower-cost crab.
habitat: Sandy and seagrass areas in shallow coastal waters
flavour: Delicate, sweet and clean meat
texture: Lighter and flakier than mud crab; less meat per gram
preparation: Sweetest steamed simply or stir-fried with salted egg or ginger and spring onion. Males (blue-tinged, blue claws) have firmer meat; females carry roe. Best cooked the day you buy it.
description: Lighter crab with long swimming-paddle hind legs and a beautifully mottled blue-and-white (males) or mottled brown (females) shell with spiny edges and long slender claws.

iucnNote: Not formally evaluated by the IUCN. A by-catch delicacy increasingly popular in zi char.
habitat: Burrows in sandy and muddy seabeds
flavour: Very sweet, somewhere between prawn and lobster
preparation: Deep-fry then toss with garlic and chilli, or salted egg. Snip down both sides of the underside shell with scissors to free the meat. Handle with care — the front limbs are sharp.
description: Long flattened body resembling a cross between a prawn and a mantis, with large folding raptorial front claws and a segmented, armoured tail fan. Pale grey with faint banding.

iucnNote: Data Deficient — fast-growing and short-lived, generally considered resilient, but not formally assessed.
habitat: Coastal waters and seagrass beds
flavour: Sweet and briny
texture: Springy and tender if cooked fast, rubbery if overcooked
preparation: Clean by pulling out the head and the clear plastic-like quill. Score the inside of the body in a crosshatch so it curls. Cook hard and fast (under 2 minutes) or braise long and slow — anything in between turns it rubbery.
description: Torpedo-shaped translucent-to-pinkish body with long fins running almost the full length of the mantle, two long tentacles and eight arms. Sold whole and glossy.

iucnNote: Data Deficient — a major fishery species across the region that has not been formally assessed.
habitat: Sandy and muddy coastal seabeds
flavour: Sweet and more deeply savoury than squid
texture: Thicker and meatier than squid; pleasantly chewy
preparation: Remove the hard internal cuttlebone and ink sac (keep the ink for black sauces). Its thick mantle is great scored and stir-fried with sambal or kang kong, or blanched for Teochew-style cold cuttlefish.
description: Broad, flattened oval body, thicker than a squid, with a fringing fin around the entire mantle and a hard internal cuttlebone. Often has a zebra-like banding when fresh.

iucnNote: Least Concern; short-lived and fast-growing. Less common in wet markets than squid.
habitat: Reefs and rocky coastal areas
flavour: Sweet and mineral, deeper than squid
preparation: Tenderise by simmering gently for 30–45 minutes (a wine cork in the pot is the old trick) before grilling or stir-frying. Rinse the suckers well. Slice thin for salads.
description: Soft bulbous head (mantle) with eight long arms lined with two rows of suckers, and no internal shell. Mottled reddish-brown, able to change colour.

iucnNote: Not formally evaluated by the IUCN; extensively farmed on mudflats across the region.
habitat: Intertidal mudflats; farmed on tidal beds
flavour: Rich, irony and intensely 'of the sea' (the red 'blood' juice)
preparation: The char kway teow and laksa essential. Blanch very briefly in just-boiled water (10–20 seconds) so they stay plump and juicy — overcooking makes them tough and rubbery. Always source from clean, reputable suppliers and cook thoroughly enough to be safe.
description: Small, heart-shaped shells with deep ribbed grooves radiating across the surface, off-white to grey. Named for the reddish fluid (haemoglobin) inside.

iucnNote: Least Concern; widely farmed on ropes and a low-impact, sustainable protein.
habitat: Farmed on ropes and posts in coastal waters
flavour: Mild, sweet and briny
preparation: Scrub the shells and pull off the wiry 'beard'. Discard any that stay open when tapped raw, or stay closed after cooking. Steam with garlic, chilli and Thai basil, or simmer in curry. Cooks in 4–5 minutes.
description: Elongated teardrop shell with a vivid emerald-green margin fading to brown, and a smooth, glossy surface. Often sold live in mesh bags.

iucnNote: Not formally evaluated; farmed oysters are filter feeders and among the most sustainable seafood.
sizeWeight: Sold whole or shucked by weight
habitat: Farmed on coastal beds and racks
flavour: Briny, creamy and umami-rich
preparation: Small shucked oysters are the heart of orh luak (oyster omelette). Rinse gently to remove grit. For omelettes, fold in near the end so they stay juicy. Larger ones can be steamed with garlic or eaten raw if sashimi-grade and very fresh.
description: Rough, craggy grey shells with an irregular teardrop shape; the meat is plump, beige and glistening. Often sold pre-shucked in tubs for cooking.

iucnNote: Not formally evaluated; widely farmed and a quick, sustainable weeknight seafood.
habitat: Sandy and muddy intertidal flats; farmed
flavour: Sweet, clean and briny
preparation: Soak in salted water 1–2 hours so they spit out sand. The zi char classic: stir-fry hard and fast with sliced chilli, garlic and a splash of wine until they just open. Discard any that don't open.
description: Small oval shells with fine concentric ridges and pretty mottled tan, grey and brown patterns. Sold live and tightly closed (or snapping shut when handled).

iucnNote: Not formally evaluated; hand-gathered or farmed. A prized restaurant-style shellfish.
habitat: Burrows vertically in intertidal sand
flavour: Sweet, rich and a little more intense than clams
preparation: Soak to purge sand. Steam quickly with minced garlic, glass noodles and light soy, or with garlic and Chinese wine — they cook in 3–4 minutes and toughen fast. Trim off the dark digestive tip before eating.
description: Long, narrow tube-shaped shells like an old straight razor or a stick of bamboo, golden-brown and slightly curved, with the meat protruding from both ends.
